meta.title: BMW 8 Series E31 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: BMW 8 Series E31 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E31 8 Series — from the 4.0-litre M60 V8 to the 5.6-litre S70 V12 that powered the 850CSi. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's flagship V8 and V12 grand tourer. Backed by 120+ real UK E31 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 8 Series E31 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E31 8 Series — spanning the M60 and M62 V8s, and the M70, M73 and S70 V12s that defined BMW's flagship grand tourer.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages. The M60 and M62 V8s are considered reliable when properly maintained, though cooling system components (water pump, radiator) and Nikasil cylinder lining issues on early M60s are well-documented concerns. The M70 V12 is a robust, over-engineered unit but can suffer from distributor cap and rotor issues, and fuel system problems. The M73 is an improvement over the M70 with better engine management. The S70B56 in the 850CSi is a motorsport-derived masterpiece — reliable for a high-performance V12 but complex and expensive to maintain.

overview.intro: The E31 is the first generation of the BMW 8 Series, unveiled at the 1989 Frankfurt Motor Show. Production began in February 1990 and continued until May 1999. Designed as BMW's flagship grand tourer, the E31 was powered by a range of naturally aspirated V8 and V12 engines — a deliberate move away from the inline-six engines that had powered BMW's coupes for decades. The range initially launched with the M70-powered 850i V12, followed by the V8-powered 840Ci in 1993. A significant mid-life update brought the M73 5.4-litre V12 and M62 4.4-litre V8, while the S70-powered 850CSi remained the range-topping performance variant until production ended in 1996.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E31 (Z1-based chassis) • Predecessor: E24 6 Series (not direct successor) • Successor: G15 8 Series (2018) • Sibling body styles: None (single 2-door coupé body style).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E31 8 Series generated 120+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the V8 840Ci and V12 850Ci as classic car buyers seek BMW's flagship grand tourer.

items.quote: The M70 is a robust, over-engineered unit but age has taken its toll — distributor cap and rotor issues, fuel system problems, and cooling system failures are common on 30+ year-old examples. The early engine management system is less sophisticated than the M73's.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a budget who are willing to invest in maintenance.

items.quote: The S70 is the most expensive engine to maintain and replace in the E31 range — specialist parts are scarce, and a full rebuild can exceed £14,000. The 850CSi is a future classic, but it comes with six-figure maintenance expectations.
items.whoItsFor: collectors with deep pockets and specialist support.

items.quote: The M60-powered 840Ci is the most affordable entry point to E31 ownership — V8 running costs are significantly lower than the V12s, and the M60 is a proven engine (though check for Nikasil lining issues on early cars). It offers 90% of the 850i experience at a fraction of the price.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want E31 ownership without V12 running costs.

items.quote: The M73 is often overshadowed by the S70 — but it's the smart V12 buy. More power than the M70 (322 bhp vs 296), better engine management (Siemens OBD II), and significantly more reliable than its predecessor. It's the V12 to buy if you can't stretch to an 850CSi.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V12 refinement with modern(ish) engine management.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Water pump, timing chain guides, VANOS
Distributor caps, rotors, fuel system, cooling
Cooling system, ignition coils, fuel injectors
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best V12 — more reliable than M70.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Rod bearings, camshaft wear, specialist parts
rows.ownershipVerdict: Ultimate V12 — collectable, costly to maintain.

economicsRule.title: The E31 rule
economicsRule.text: A £5,000–£8,000 M70 recon against a £8,000–£25,000 E31 sits at 20–100% of the car's value【BMW-QUOTE】. On a sub-£12,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to 25–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For 850CSi cars (£30,000–£60,000+), even the most expensive S70 replacement is viable given collector values.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E31 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M60/M62 840Ci — V8 running costs are significantly lower than the V12s.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E31 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S70 850CSi — parts scarcity and V12 complexity make this the most expensive E31 to maintain.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E31 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M73 850Ci (post-1994) — V12 refinement with more reliable engine management than the M70.

problems.title: M60 V8 Nikasil Cylinder Lining Wear
problems.rootCause: Early M60 V8s used Nikasil (nickel-silicon carbide) cylinder linings which could degrade when exposed to high-sulphur fuels. This led to loss of compression, increased oil consumption, and eventual engine failure. BMW issued a recall and replaced affected engines with Alusil-lined units.

tieredCosts.work: Full M60 engine replacement or rebuild

problems.recommendation: If you're buying an M60-powered 840Ci (1993–1995), get a compression test and borescope inspection before committing — £1,000–£2,000 now is far cheaper than the £4,500+ engine replacement that follows if the Nikasil linings have failed. Check the vehicle's service history for evidence of recall work.

cta.label: Compare M60 engine prices for your 840Ci →

problems.title: M70 V12 Distributor Cap & Rotor Failure
problems.rootCause: The M70 V12 uses two distributor caps and rotors — one for each bank of cylinders. These components degrade with age and heat, leading to misfires, rough running, and reduced performance. The caps and rotors are becoming increasingly difficult to source.

tieredCosts.work: Full M70 engine replacement if misfire caused damage

problems.recommendation: Replace the distributor caps and rotors every 40,000 miles or 5 years — a £500–£1,000 specialist job is cheap insurance against a £4,500+ engine replacement that follows if misfire damage occurs. Keep a spare set if you plan to keep the car long-term — these parts are getting harder to find.

cta.label: Compare M70 engine parts for your 850i →

problems.title: E31 Cooling System Failure (All Engines)
problems.affectedModels: All E31 models (M60, M62, M70, M73, S70).
problems.rootCause: The E31's cooling system uses plastic components that become brittle with age — water pumps, radiators, expansion tanks and hoses are all potential failure points. Overheating can cause catastrophic engine damage on both V8 and V12 engines.

tieredCosts.work: Water pump, thermostat, radiator, hoses replacement
Full engine replacement if overheating caused damage

problems.recommendation: Replace the entire cooling system (water pump, thermostat, radiator, expansion tank, hoses) as preventative maintenance at 60,000 miles or 10 years — a £600–£1,500 specialist job is dramatically cheaper than the £4,500+ engine replacement that follows an overheating event. This is the single most important maintenance item on any E31.

problems.title: E31 Fuel System & Injector Issues (All Engines)
problems.affectedModels: All E31 models, particularly V12s (M70, M73, S70).
problems.rootCause: The E31's fuel system is complex — the V12s have two separate fuel rails and two fuel pumps. Fuel injectors can become clogged or fail with age, leading to rough running, poor economy, and potential engine damage. The fuel pumps are also a known weak point.

tieredCosts.work: Fuel injector cleaning/replacement, fuel pump replacement
Full engine replacement if fuel starvation caused damage

problems.recommendation: Have the fuel injectors cleaned or replaced at 60,000 miles — a £500–£1,500 specialist job is far cheaper than the £4,500+ engine replacement that follows if fuel starvation causes a lean-running failure. On V12 cars, budget for two fuel pumps if they haven't been replaced.

replacementCosts.h2: E31 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the EM_Proprietary_Data baseline figures — E31 is a unique generation with no direct successor cost comparison. Costs are derived from specialist market data and tagged [BMW-QUOTE].

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E31 8 Series only. The 840Ci switched from the M60B40 (1993–1995) to the M62B44 (1995–1999). The 850i (1990–1994) used the M70B50; the 850Ci (1994–1999) used the M73B54. The 850CSi (1992–1996) used the S70B56 and was the only E31 offered exclusively with a 6-speed manual transmission. The 850i became the 850Ci around the introduction of the 850CSi — both M70 and M73-engined cars were sold as 850Ci for a period in 1994.
